executive_summary: The US dollar trades at DXY ~99.0, temporarily buoyed by safe-haven demand following the US-Iran war launched February 28 but structurally weakened by a closing US-ROW growth differential, a stagflationary data mix (core PCE 3.0% YoY alongside NFP of -92K), and the Fed's paralysis at 3.50–3.75%. DXY has rallied ~3% from its pre-war 96 handle to touch 99.68 — its highest since late January — but remains well below its 200-day MA (~101–103) and down approximately 8–10% over the past year. The US exceptionalism narrative that powered USD through 2024 has been decisively undermined: S&P Global's composite PMI differential between the US and Eurozone has collapsed to zero for the first time since April 2024, while MSCI ex-US equities outperformed the S&P 500 by more than 10 percentage points YTD (pre-war). Aggregate CFTC net speculative positioning reached -$22.8B — the most bearish since March 2021 — before Iran-war short-covering trimmed it to -$19.6B. Our stance is tactically neutral (safe-haven bid offsets structural bearishness) but strategically bearish as twin deficits at ~8.8% of GDP, the loss of all AAA sovereign ratings, and the approaching Powell-to-Warsh Fed Chair transition compound with fading rate differentials to resume the cyclical USD downtrend once geopolitical risk premia dissipate. The March 18 FOMC meeting with fresh dot plot is the next decisive catalyst.

spot.convention: Rising DXY = USD strengthening vs G10 basket; falling DXY = USD weakening
spot.primary_index: DXY (US Dollar Index) ~99.0 — trade-weighted basket (EUR 57.6%, JPY 13.6%, GBP 11.9%, CAD 9.1%, SEK 4.2%, CHF 3.6%)

bis_reer.interpretation: The REER has corrected approximately 13% from the cycle peak of 122.6 (January 2025) but remains ~7% above the 2020 base of 100, indicating moderate residual overvaluation on a trade-weighted basis.

valuation.trade_weighted_assessment: The BIS REER at 106.9 confirms USD has corrected substantially from extreme overvaluation but retains a moderate premium reflecting reserve currency status and higher yields versus most G10 peers.
valuation.what_is_priced_in: The BIS REER decline from 122.6 to 106.9 has priced in much of the US exceptionalism unwind. The remaining overvaluation (~7% above base) is consistent with USD retaining a premium from reserve currency status and higher yields. This residual premium is vulnerable if the twin deficit narrative gains traction or if the Fed cuts more aggressively than priced.
valuation.actionability: The PPP signal is structurally bearish USD but is a slow-moving gravity — not an actionable tactical signal. Most actionable bilaterally: USD/JPY (extreme PPP deviation + BoJ tightening) and USD/CHF (extreme undervaluation, but driven by safe-haven flows that may persist).

cftc.percentile_context: Aggregate net USD shorts at ~85th percentile of bearishness on 5-year lookback, trimmed from ~95th percentile two weeks prior (pre-war extreme).

positioning_summary.other_positioning_signals: UUP (USD Bull ETF) saw +$53.6M 5-day inflows and +$80.7M monthly inflows — strongest bullish flow since mid-2024. UDN (USD Bear ETF) saw -$3.6M outflows. JPY open interest surged +47,256 contracts in one week — largest increase in over a year.
positioning_summary.interpretation: The war-driven short-covering is reducing the extreme net-short USD position but has not reversed it — aggregate positioning remains firmly USD-bearish. The short-covering looks tactical and forced (risk reduction) rather than a genuine reversal of the structural bearish consensus. A ceasefire or de-escalation would likely see the short-USD trade rebuild.
positioning_summary.contrarian_assessment: The remaining ~-$19.6B net short represents a crowded trade that creates asymmetric risk: if the war escalates beyond current levels (e.g., full Strait of Hormuz closure), a violent short squeeze toward DXY 102–104 is possible. Conversely, rapid de-escalation removes the only prop supporting USD against its structural headwinds.

market_structure_vol_summary.trend_regime: High-volatility counter-trend rally within structural downtrend (bearish medium-term, war-driven bounce)

moving_averages.alignment: Bearishly stacked (200d > 100d > 50d; spot below all three)

volatility.rv_vs_iv: RV ~13.5% vs IV ~12.0% — spot moving more than options anticipated; favours spot over options expressions
volatility.risk_reversal_25d_1m: Modestly USD-call-bid (positive), reflecting war-driven safe-haven hedging demand, though structural flow favours USD puts
volatility.vol_regime: High-vol counter-trend (war premium + FOMC event cluster creating elevated near-term vol)

market_structure_vol_summary.key_observation: The Iran war has created a high-vol counter-trend environment. DXY is rallying within a structural downtrend, and the rally's sustainability depends entirely on the geopolitical outcome. The 200-day MA at 101–103 is the line in the sand — a close above it would challenge the bearish structural thesis.

market_context.recent_regime: The USD has been in a structural weakening phase since mid-2025, driven by the unwinding of US exceptionalism, narrowing rate differentials (three Fed cuts in H2 2025), and massive European fiscal stimulus (Germany's €500B infrastructure programme). DXY fell from ~110 (January 2025 highs) to ~96 (late February 2026) — a 13% decline. The US-Iran war launched February 28 abruptly interrupted this trend, generating a safe-haven bid that pushed DXY to 99.68 and temporarily repriced Fed cut expectations from two cuts to one. The dominant regime is now a tug-of-war between geopolitical safe-haven demand (bullish USD tactically) and the structural forces that were driving USD lower (bearish USD strategically).

key_spreads.interpretation: Compressed from +180 six months ago; narrowing supports EUR/USD
Narrowing as BoJ hikes; structural headwind for USD/JPY bulls
Gilt yields above UST — rare; reflects UK inflation premium
ACGB above UST; RBA hawkishness widens this gap
Significant; reflects US fiscal premium over Canada
Near-zero Swiss yields; massive differential reflects SNB zero bound
NZ yields above UST despite lower policy rate; curve premium
Norwegian yields slightly above UST; Norges Bank at 4.00%
Reflects MAS S$NEER management and low local rates

sovereign_yields.curve_shape: UST 2s10s at +57–62 bps (positive, steepening). Reflects returning term premium from fiscal supply concerns rather than growth optimism. Steepening is marginally bearish USD.

tactical.analysis: The Fed Funds rate at 3.50–3.75% ranks the USD 3rd in G10+SGD behind Norway (4.00%) and a virtual tie with Australia (3.85%) and the UK (3.75%), providing a moderate carry advantage against most peers. The UST-Bund 10Y spread at +130bps and UST-JGB spread at +207bps still offer meaningful yield pickup for holding USD-denominated assets, and the Iran war has reinforced this by drawing flight-to-quality flows into Treasuries. However, the Fed is trapped in a stagflationary bind: core PCE at 3.0% prevents cutting, while NFP of -92K and JOLTS at 6.54M argue for easing. The March 18 FOMC with fresh dot plot is one week away, with CME FedWatch pricing 92–97% probability of a hold and the first cut not fully priced until June (47% probability). The key tactical question is whether the new dot plot shifts to zero 2026 cuts (hawkish, USD rally) or holds at one cut with dovish labour market language (limited DXY impact). The rate differential trajectory is narrowing on the margin — UST-Bund 10Y compressed from +180bps to +130bps over six months. The 2Y yield at 3.557% versus 2Y Bund at ~2.15% still offers +140bps of front-end carry through the tactical horizon.
key_data_points: Fed Funds target range: 3.50–3.75% (effective ~3.64%), 3rd highest in G10+SGD
UST 2Y: 3.557%; UST 10Y: 4.174%; 2s10s: +57–62 bps (positive)
UST-Bund 10Y spread: +130 bps (compressed from +180 bps 6 months ago)
UST-JGB 10Y spread: +207 bps (narrowing as BoJ tightens)
March FOMC hold probability: 92–97%; first full cut priced for June at 47%
OIS-implied year-end 2026 rate: ~3.15–3.25% (two 25bp cuts)
December dot plot median 2026 YE: 3.4% (one cut) — market ~25bps more dovish
SOFR: 3.65%, spread to IORB ~0 bps (no dislocation)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices approximately 50bps of cumulative cuts by year-end (two 25bp reductions), slightly more dovish than the December dot plot median of one cut. The market is not pricing the risk that the March dot plot eliminates all 2026 cuts given tariff-driven inflation concern and ISM Prices Paid at 70.5. If the new dots show zero cuts as median, USD rates would reprice higher and DXY could rally 1–2% tactically.

strategic.analysis: Over the 1–6 month horizon, the rate differential trajectory favours USD weakness. The Fed's December SEP projects the funds rate falling to 3.4% by year-end 2026, and the stagflationary data mix — particularly if the labor market continues deteriorating (NFP has been negative in 3 of the last 5 months) — will eventually force the Fed's hand. Meanwhile, the RBA has hiked to 3.85% and is expected to reach 4.10–4.35%, Norges Bank holds at 4.00%, and BoJ is expected to hike to 1.00% by June. The bilateral rate differentials that underpinned USD strength are eroding from both sides: the Fed is heading lower while several G10 peers are heading higher or holding. The UST-Bund 10Y spread at +130bps is likely to compress further toward +100bps as German infrastructure spending boosts Bund yields. The UST-JGB spread at +207bps will continue narrowing as BoJ normalises — a decline toward +150bps over 6 months is plausible, pressuring USD/JPY significantly. The critical risk is if inflation re-accelerates sharply, forcing the Fed back toward a hawkish stance. The Kevin Warsh nomination as Powell's successor adds uncertainty — his confirmation process could create policy uncertainty through the transition period.
key_data_points: December dot plot median 2026 YE: 3.4% (3.25–3.50% implied range)
Dot plot range: 2.1% to 3.9% (180bps dispersion among FOMC participants)
RBA expected peak: 4.10–4.35% (rising USD spread compression vs AUD)
BoJ expected by June: 1.00% (UST-JGB 10Y narrowing from +207 toward +150)
UST-Bund 10Y projected: +100 bps by September (from current +130)
Fed net interest expense: $970B/year (~19% of federal revenue)
Powell term expiration: May 15, 2026; Warsh nomination pending Senate confirmation
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices a terminal rate around 3.15–3.25% by end-2026, broadly consistent with the dot plot median but with a more front-loaded cut timeline. The market underprices two scenarios: (1) the Fed is forced to cut earlier and more aggressively if labor market deterioration accelerates, which would be USD-negative; and (2) the Fed is forced to hold or hike if tariff-driven inflation re-accelerates, which would be USD-positive but damaging to growth. The net edge is modestly bearish because the growth deterioration appears more advanced than the market acknowledges.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: The tactical neutral stance versus strategic bearish reflects the timing mismatch between geopolitical and fundamental forces. Tactically, the Iran war and upcoming March FOMC keep USD bid on safe-haven demand and policy uncertainty. Strategically, the structural forces — narrowing rate differentials, closing growth gap, twin deficit, Fed Chair transition — dominate as geopolitical risk premia fade.

carry_vs_peers.interpretation: Positive carry for holding USD vs EUR; moderate attractiveness
Largest G10 carry advantage; historically significant for USD/JPY
Highest carry-to-vol ratio; most attractive carry pair on paper
Negative carry — USD now pays to hold vs GBP
Negative carry — RBA hike has flipped the carry direction
Negative carry — Norges Bank at 4.00% exceeds Fed
Positive carry; BoC at 2.25% well below Fed

carry_analysis.carry_regime: Carry has been a net loser in 2026 as the risk-off regime driven by the Iran war has overwhelmed carry dynamics. The yen — the lowest-yielding G10 currency — has weakened despite carry logic because USD/JPY is driven more by rate differentials and risk sentiment than by pure carry.
carry_analysis.breakeven_move: For USD/JPY at +288bps annualised carry, the breakeven is approximately 35 pips over 4 weeks — trivially small relative to daily ranges of 100+ pips in the current volatility environment.

tactical.analysis: The US data flow presents a textbook stagflationary signal that is unambiguously negative for the dollar. February NFP printed -92K — the third contraction in five months — with the unemployment rate rising to 4.4% and average duration of unemployment hitting 25.7 weeks (the longest since December 2021). JOLTS job openings at 6.542M are at post-pandemic lows, confirming labor demand is cooling rapidly. Yet inflation remains sticky: core PCE jumped to 3.0% YoY in December, ISM Manufacturing Prices Paid surged to 70.5 (up 11.5 points), and ISM Imports hit 54.9 — the highest since February 2022 — signalling tariff-driven front-loading. The US exceptionalism narrative has been materially damaged: S&P Global US Composite PMI at 51.9 has converged to the Eurozone's 51.9, closing a gap that averaged +3 points for over two years. Q4 2025 GDP disappointed at +1.4% SAAR versus 3.0% consensus, while Atlanta Fed GDPNow has been revised from 3.1% to 2.1%. Consumer sentiment is bifurcated — Conference Board at 91.2 while University of Michigan collapsed to 56.6.
key_data_points: February NFP: -92,000 (consensus was +80K; 3rd decline in 5 months)
Unemployment rate: 4.4% (up from 4.3%); U-6: 7.9%
Core PCE YoY: 3.0% (up from 2.8%); Core CPI YoY: 2.5% (Jan)
ISM Manufacturing Prices Paid: 70.5 (up 11.5pp — largest single-month surge in 2 years)
Q4 2025 GDP: +1.4% SAAR (vs 3.0% consensus); GDPNow Q1 2026: 2.1%
JOLTS job openings: 6.542M (lowest since September 2020)
US vs Eurozone Composite PMI: 51.9 vs 51.9 (gap closed to zero)
Average hourly earnings: +3.8% YoY
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has partially priced the growth slowdown — DXY fell from 110 to 96 over the past year. However, the market has NOT priced the stagflationary scenario where growth continues to weaken while inflation re-accelerates. The ISM Prices Paid at 70.5 alongside NFP of -92K is not reflected in Fed funds futures, which still price a gradual, benign easing cycle. If February CPI surprises upside today, the stagflation trade would intensify.

strategic.analysis: The strategic growth picture for the US has deteriorated markedly relative to the rest of the world, and this convergence is the single most important driver of strategic USD weakness. The December 2025 SEP projected 2.3% GDP growth for 2026, but the actual trajectory suggests downside risk. The labor market is the key transmission channel: if NFP continues negative, the unemployment rate could rise toward 4.8–5.0% by mid-year, forcing the Fed to cut more aggressively than currently priced. The inflation picture is more ambiguous strategically — the Supreme Court's IEEPA ruling reduced the effective tariff rate, and if the tariff pass-through fades by mid-year, core PCE could glide toward 2.5% by Q3. The Euro area growth momentum is improving — German fiscal stimulus, Eurozone Composite PMI back above 50 — which further compresses the US-ROW growth differential. The Iran war's oil price impact could keep input costs elevated and prevent the disinflation necessary for rate cuts. Canada's contraction suggests USD/CAD may be less responsive to the broad USD bear thesis.
key_data_points: December SEP GDP projection for 2026: 2.3% (likely to be revised lower in March dots)
NFP negative in 3 of last 5 months (trend deterioration)
Fed SEP unemployment projection 2026: 4.4% (already reached — forecast obsolete)
Core PCE trajectory: 3.0% → projected 2.5% by Q3 if tariff pass-through fades
WTI crude: $83–91 (each $10 increase adds ~0.1–0.2pp to headline CPI over 3 months)
Eurozone Composite PMI: 51.9 (matched US for first time since April 2024)
German infrastructure spending plan: €500B over 10 years
strategic.what_is_priced: Bank consensus overwhelmingly projects EUR/USD at 1.20–1.25 by year-end, pricing ~50bps of Fed cuts and gradual differential narrowing. The edge is that labor market deterioration may force faster/deeper cuts than the base case, particularly if the war ends and oil retraces — that combination would give the Fed room to cut and accelerate USD weakness. The risk is persistent stagflation keeping the Fed on hold while growth deteriorates.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical and strategic stances align (both bearish), but tactical confidence is higher (65 vs 60) because the near-term data flow is unambiguously USD-negative (today's CPI, March 18 FOMC), while the strategic picture carries more two-sided risk from the war's inflation pass-through and the Fed's reaction function uncertainty.

tactical.analysis: The risk environment is firmly risk-off and this is tactically USD-positive given the dollar's position on the left side of the dollar smile. The VIX at 22.8–25.5 has spiked from pre-war levels of ~17–20, with an intraday peak of 35.3 during the initial Iran strikes. The S&P 500 is flat-to-slightly-negative YTD. Credit markets have been remarkably resilient — US IG OAS at ~77–81bps remains near multi-decade tights — suggesting risk-off is concentrated in equities, commodities, and FX rather than credit. Gold has surged to all-time highs at $5,130–5,230, competing with USD for safe-haven flows. The standard correlation structure is intact: VIX up → DXY up → equities down → gold up. USD sits on the left side of the dollar smile (strong in global crisis). The cross-currency basis swaps have likely widened on increased USD funding demand — a direct USD-positive signal.
key_data_points: VIX: 22.8–25.5 (peaked 35.3; pre-war ~17–20); above 75th percentile on 1Y
S&P 500: ~6,740–6,827 (flat YTD; pre-war YTD +0.7%)
US IG OAS: ~77–81 bps (near multi-decade tights, ~10th percentile of wideness)
US HY OAS: ~319 bps (widened ~50bps from late-2025 tights)
Gold: $5,130–5,230 (all-time highs; +79% YoY)
MSCI EAFE YTD: +10.1% vs S&P 500 YTD: +0.7% (9.4pp US underperformance)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the initial war shock but has NOT priced a prolonged conflict scenario. If the Strait of Hormuz is fully closed, VIX could spike to 40+ and DXY would rally to 102–104. Conversely, a ceasefire within 2–4 weeks would collapse the risk-off premium, taking DXY back toward 96–97. The downside from de-escalation (~3–4%) exceeds the upside from escalation (~2–3%) because the short-USD positioning remains crowded.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the risk regime is expected to shift from risk-off back toward the bottom of the dollar smile. The Iran war's market impact, while severe, is likely to be transitory — historical precedents show FX markets typically reprice within 1–3 months of the initial shock. The structural forces driving the global risk-on rotation into ex-US assets remain intact and would reassert once geopolitical risk premia fade. S&P 500 underperformance versus MSCI ex-US preceded the conflict and reflects genuine relative growth deceleration and tech multiple compression. Credit spreads at historically tight levels suggest the corporate sector is healthy, supporting risk appetite once the geopolitical overlay dissipates. Confidence is capped at 50% because the war's duration is unknowable and an escalation scenario would keep USD on the left side of the smile for longer.
key_data_points: Gulf War 1991: DXY gave back safe-haven gains within 2 months of ceasefire
MSCI EM YTD: +14.4% (pre-war); structural rotation into EM equities ongoing
Copper at ~$5.85–6.00/lb (near record; AI demand-driven — structural growth indicator)
Bitcoin: ~$69K (down 45% from ATH; crypto-specific deleveraging)
US IG OAS: ~77–81 bps (near multi-decade tights — corporate health signal)
strategic.what_is_priced: The market is pricing a moderate risk-off premium in USD (DXY ~99 vs fundamental fair value ~95–96). If the war ends within 3 months, DXY would return to the 95–97 range. The strategic edge is that the market is overpricing the persistence of the war premium and underpricing the speed of the rotation back into ex-US assets once geopolitical risk clears.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: The tactical bullish versus strategic bearish split is driven entirely by the dollar smile framework. Tactically, the war puts USD on the left side (strong in crisis). Strategically, the base case is that the war resolves or is discounted, returning USD to the bottom of the smile (weak in goldilocks). The inflection occurs when either a ceasefire is announced or markets stop reacting to war headlines (conflict fatigue, typically 4–8 weeks from onset).

tactical.analysis: Positioning dynamics are tactically USD-positive despite the aggregate net-short USD position. The key signal is the direction of change: aggregate net speculative USD shorts have been trimmed from -$22.8B (~95th percentile bearishness on 5-year lookback) to -$19.6B in the latest report. This $3.2B reduction represents aggressive war-driven short-covering that could accelerate if the war escalates. UUP saw +$53.6M of 5-day inflows and +$80.7M monthly — the sharpest bullish flow since mid-2024. EUR/USD asset manager longs at +370K contracts are declining from near-record levels, suggesting smart money is taking profit. JPY open interest surged +47K contracts — the largest weekly increase in over a year. GBP asset manager shorts at -125K contracts represent a significant bearish bet that could squeeze. TIC data shows continued foreign selling of Treasuries — Japan -$17.2B, China -$82B over 9 months — structurally USD-negative but slower timescale.
key_data_points: Aggregate net USD spec positioning: -$19.6B (from -$22.8B; ~85th percentile bearish)
Weekly change: +$3.2B (aggressive short-covering)
EUR/USD asset manager longs: +370,272 contracts (declining from near-record)
UUP 5-day inflows: +$53.6M; 1-month: +$80.7M (strongest since mid-2024)
JPY open interest change: +47,256 (largest weekly increase in >1 year)
China UST holdings: $683.5B (down $82B in 9 months)
IMF COFER USD share: 56.92% (30-year low)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the initial war-driven short-covering, but the remaining ~-$19.6B aggregate short is still a crowded position. The tactical risk is that further escalation triggers a disorderly short squeeze taking DXY 2–3% higher than fundamentals justify. The asymmetry favours USD bulls tactically: the crowded short means the path of least resistance for positioning flows is USD-positive.

strategic.analysis: Strategically, the positioning picture is USD-negative because the structural forces driving the short-USD consensus have not changed. The war-driven short-covering is a tactical adjustment, not a fundamental reassessment. Once the geopolitical premium fades, the short-USD trade will likely rebuild. TIC data shows declining foreign UST holdings across major holders. IMF COFER USD share at 56.92% continues a multi-decade decline that accelerated following the weaponisation of USD reserves (Russia sanctions 2022). UDN has attracted +$86.7M over the past year despite recent outflows, confirming the strategic bearish trend. Real money flows are increasingly diversifying away from USD assets — a trend the war temporarily obscured but did not reverse.
key_data_points: Aggregate USD shorts trimmed from -$22.8B to -$19.6B (likely to rebuild post-war)
TIC: Japan -$17.2B, China -$82B (9mo), India -$36.2B YoY, Brazil -$32.9B YoY
COFER USD share: 56.92% (down from 58.51% in Q1 2025)
UDN 1-year flows: +$86.7M (strategic bearish positioning intact)
CAD asset manager longs at 4.5-year high (+61,582 contracts)
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has priced a moderate USD-bearish consensus but positioning has not reached a level that constitutes reversal risk. The -$19.6B aggregate short is well below the -$30B+ extremes seen in 2017–2018. The edge is that structural positioning flows (reserve diversification, TIC selling) will overwhelm tactical safe-haven repositioning once the war discount is fully embedded.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bullish versus strategic bearish positioning reflects the forced nature of the short-covering. Tactically, the crowded short creates squeeze risk. Strategically, the structural forces driving the short-USD consensus are intact and will reassert once the war premium fades.
factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: The aggregate net-short USD position at -$19.6B (~85th percentile bearish) is stretched enough to generate a meaningful squeeze if triggered. A Strait of Hormuz closure could produce a disorderly short squeeze of 3–5% in DXY (to 102–104), analogous to the March 2020 dash-for-cash. The mechanism: forced liquidation of EUR/USD longs → dealer hedging → stops triggered at DXY 100 → CTA buying above 200-day MA. The other side of the trade sees war-driven USD strength as temporary and the structural bear thesis intact — they are correct directionally but vulnerable to violent short-term squeezes.
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: The consensus is overwhelmingly bearish USD (banks targeting EUR/USD 1.20–1.25 year-end). The contrarian scenario is that US stagflation prevents the Fed from cutting, while European growth disappoints fiscal stimulus expectations, re-widening rate differentials and pushing EUR/USD back toward 1.10. Probability: 20%. This requires both US inflation re-acceleration above 3.5% PCE and European growth disappointment. The historical analogue is 2018 when consensus was bearish USD but tax reform + Fed hiking cycle reversed the trade.

tactical.analysis: The external balance provides mixed signals at the tactical horizon. The US current account deficit at 2.9% of GDP and goods trade deficit at -$99.3B are structurally USD-negative but slow-moving. The tactical relevance comes from the terms of trade shock: WTI surged from $63 to $83–91. The US is a net energy exporter, so rising oil has a mildly positive terms of trade effect — unlike pre-2015. However, the oil shock's growth/inflation impact is net-negative for USD because it exacerbates stagflation. The twin deficit at ~8.8% of GDP is the widest in G10+SGD but operates on strategic timeframes. The Supreme Court's IEEPA ruling has modestly improved the trade balance outlook. USD's dominant role in global trade invoicing (~96% in Americas) provides structural demand. Net: the oil spike is mildly USD-positive through terms of trade but mildly USD-negative through inflation/growth, and these effects largely offset.
key_data_points: Current account: -$226.4B, -2.9% of GDP (Q3 2025)
Goods trade deficit: -$99.3B (December 2025)
NIIP: -$27.61T (record); ~-88% of GDP
Twin deficit: ~8.8% of GDP (fiscal 5.9% + CA 2.9%)
USD trade invoicing share: 96% Americas, 74% Asia-Pacific
tactical.what_is_priced: The current account deficit is fully reflected in the structural USD discount accumulated over the past year. The oil spike is partially priced through terms of trade effects but the second-round growth impact of sustained oil above $80 is not fully priced. Net: no actionable tactical edge from external balance.

strategic.analysis: At the strategic horizon, external balance is USD's most significant structural vulnerability. The twin deficit at ~8.8% of GDP is unsustainable without sustained foreign capital inflows, and the evidence suggests those inflows are weakening. TIC data shows Japan, China, India, and Brazil all reducing UST holdings. The NIIP at -$27.61T is a record. Several catalysts could activate the twin deficit narrative over 6 months: debt ceiling around November 2026, CBO projections showing debt/GDP surpassing WWII levels by 2029, and the Moody's downgrade removing the last AAA rating. The depreciation of the BIS REER from 122.6 to 106.9 has partially corrected the overvaluation, but PPP suggests USD remains overvalued against most peers. The external balance factor is the single strongest contributor to the strategic bearish thesis because it operates at precisely the timeframe where current account flows influence FX.
key_data_points: Twin deficit: ~8.8% of GDP (widest in G10+SGD)
NIIP: -$27.61T (~-88% of GDP, record)
Full-year 2025 trade deficit: $901.5B
TIC net sellers: Japan (-$17.2B Dec), China (-$82B 9mo), India (-$36.2B YoY)
COFER USD share: 56.92% (30-year low)
Eurozone CA: +3.0% GDP; Japan CA: +4.4% GDP; Switzerland CA: +7.0% GDP; Singapore CA: +18.0% GDP
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has partially priced external balance weakness through the DXY decline from 110 to 99, but bank consensus suggests another 5–8% of DXY downside. The edge is that TIC selling may accelerate — particularly from China, where the effective tariff rate of 47.5% reduces the incentive to recycle trade surpluses into USTs. A shift from passive to active UST selling by China would be a powerful additional USD headwind.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral versus strategic bearish reflects the different timeframes over which external balance operates. Current account deficits are not tactical signals. Over 1–6 months, cumulative balance of payments flows, declining foreign UST demand, and twin deficit vulnerability become the dominant fundamental driver.

tactical.analysis: The Fed's forward guidance is explicitly data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting, offering no pre-commitment the market can trade against. The January minutes revealed a deeply divided committee. The 10-2 vote (Miran and Waller dissenting for a cut) shows the dovish minority is vocal but small. The March 18 FOMC with fresh dot plot is the critical near-term communication event. The December dots showed a median of one 2026 cut but with massive 180bps dispersion, reflecting genuine disagreement. Recent Fedspeak has been mixed: Miran ultra-dovish, Waller conditionally dovish, Williams neutral, Bostic hawkish. The credibility assessment is positive — the Fed retains strong institutional credibility despite political pressure. Markets trust that the Fed will act on data rather than political pressure, which supports USD insofar as it maintains confidence in the institution.
key_data_points: January FOMC vote: 10-2 hold (Miran, Waller dissented for cut)
December dot plot median 2026 YE: 3.4% (one cut); range: 2.1–3.9% (180bps dispersion)
Market-implied 2026 YE: ~3.15–3.25% (gap: market ~25bps more dovish than dots)
Next FOMC: March 17–18 (with SEP/dot plot)
Key Fedspeak: Miran ultra-dovish (2.75% neutral), Waller coin flip, Williams eventually cut
tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices the Fed on hold through March with first cut in June at ~47%. The dot plot is the variable: if dots shift to zero cuts (hawkish surprise), USD rallies 1–2%. The market has NOT priced the tail risk that a new dot plot participant (Warsh as Chair from May 15) could materially change the policy reaction function in H2 2026.

strategic.analysis: The strategic guidance picture is USD-negative because of the approaching Fed Chair transition. Kevin Warsh, nominated to replace Powell, is perceived as potentially more hawkish on inflation but more sympathetic to the administration's growth priorities. The uncertainty surrounding the transition creates institutional risk not fully priced. A Warsh-led Fed could pursue a different communication strategy, increasing meeting-to-meeting volatility. The credibility question shifts from 'does the market trust the Fed' to 'will the market trust a Warsh Fed as much as the Powell Fed?' The December dot plot's 180bps dispersion signals no FOMC consensus, which is inherently USD-negative because policy uncertainty widens the distribution of outcomes and increases risk premium.
key_data_points: Powell term expires: May 15, 2026
Warsh nominated: January 30; Senate confirmation pending (Tillis blocking)
FOMC dot dispersion: 180bps (2.1–3.9%) — widest in current cycle
DOJ investigation of Powell: ongoing (institutional risk)
Trump attempted to fire Gov. Cook: Supreme Court case pending
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has priced a smooth, Powell-led policy path. It has NOT priced the institutional uncertainty of the Chair transition, including the possibility that Warsh signals a different reaction function. The strategic edge is that the Chair transition creates downside USD risk: either Warsh is confirmed and markets reprice the reaction function, or the confirmation is delayed and the policy vacuum creates additional uncertainty.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral versus strategic bearish reflects the different information environments. Tactically, the Fed's guidance is on autopilot (data-dependent, hold likely in March). Strategically, the Chair transition and widening FOMC disagreement create asymmetric downside risk for USD.

tactical.analysis: The US-Iran war is the dominant event risk and is tactically USD-positive. Operation Epic Fury has resulted in over 1,300 casualties in Iran, the killing of Supreme Leader Khamenei, and a threatened/partial closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Oil surged from $63 to $119 intraday before retracing to $83–91. The DXY response has been textbook safe-haven: +3% from pre-war levels. The March 18 FOMC with a fresh dot plot carries binary risk for USD rates pricing. February CPI today is a high-impact release. Other near-term events include BoJ (March 12–13), ECB (March 18–19), BoE, SNB, Riksbank (March 19) — a remarkable cluster. The Supreme Court IEEPA ruling reduced tariff uncertainty but introduced new uncertainty around Section 122 replacement.
key_data_points: Iran war: launched February 28; 1,300+ casualties; Supreme Leader killed; Hormuz threatened
WTI: pre-war $63 → current $83–91 (peak $119.48 intraday)
DXY war response: +3% (96 → 99.68)
March 18 FOMC: fresh dot plot (binary risk)
March 11 CPI: February data
Tail risk: Strait of Hormuz full closure → VIX 40+, DXY 102–104 (probability: 15%)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced a limited, relatively short war with contained oil impact (~$80–85 steady state). It has NOT priced a prolonged conflict with full Hormuz closure, which would send oil above $150 and create a 1970s-style supply shock. The CPI today is the near-term binary catalyst — a hot print would be USD-positive, cool print USD-negative.

strategic.analysis: The strategic event risk picture is highly uncertain and does not offer a clear directional bias. The war's duration is the key variable: resolution within 1–3 months removes the safe-haven bid (USD-negative); protracted conflict keeps USD supported but damages global growth and US fiscal position. USMCA review beginning July 2026 introduces trade policy risk. November 2026 midterm elections will influence policy expectations. Debt ceiling projected to be reached around November 2026. Fed Chair transition creates institutional event risk. Ukraine-Russia peace talks, if resumed, could be EUR-positive. US-China tensions remain elevated but a Trump-Xi summit is reportedly upcoming. The weight of events is roughly balanced between USD-positive and USD-negative.
key_data_points: Trump: war will end 'very soon'
USMCA review: July 2026
US midterm elections: November 3, 2026
Debt ceiling projected reached: ~November 2026
US-China effective tariff rate: 47.5%
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices a relatively benign geopolitical environment by year-end (bank consensus EUR/USD 1.20–1.25 implies DXY mid-90s, consistent with peace). The risk is symmetric: if war escalates, consensus is too bearish on USD; if war ends quickly and trade tensions de-escalate, consensus may be too cautious on speed of USD weakness.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bullish versus strategic neutral reflects the war's diminishing impact over time. The safe-haven bid is acute in the first 2–4 weeks but historically fades within 1–3 months as markets discount the conflict.

tactical.analysis: Fiscal risk is not a tactical driver. The US lost its last AAA sovereign rating when Moody's downgraded from Aaa to Aa1 in May 2025, but the market reaction was muted. Debt/GDP at 99.8% and fiscal deficit at 5.9% of GDP are elevated but long-since priced. The debt ceiling was raised to ~$40.1–41.1T and won't be reached until ~November 2026. Net interest expense at $970B (~19% of federal revenue) is at record levels but this is slow-moving. Recent Treasury auction reception has been solid — January 10Y auction saw foreign indirect bidders take 69.7%. Iran war spending will add to the deficit but is not yet quantifiable as a market factor. Tactically, fiscal risk contributes to the structural case but does not generate actionable signals over 2–4 weeks.
key_data_points: Sovereign ratings: AA+ (S&P), Aa1 (Moody's), AA+ (Fitch) — no AAA from any major agency
Debt/GDP: 99.8% (end FY2025); total debt crossed $38T
Fiscal deficit: $1.8T (5.9% of GDP, FY2025)
Net interest expense: $970B (~19% of revenue, 3.2% of GDP)
Debt ceiling: ~$40.1–41.1T; projected reached ~November 2026
January 10Y auction: indirect bidders 69.7%, bid-to-cover ~2.50
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced fiscal deterioration through UST term premium (2s10s at +57–62 bps) and the BIS REER decline. Treasury auction demand remains robust. The tactical edge is zero — fiscal risk is fully priced and unlikely to generate a catalytic event within 2–4 weeks.

strategic.analysis: Strategically, fiscal risk is a meaningful USD headwind building in intensity. The loss of all AAA ratings removes the last credibility buffer. CBO projects debt/GDP rising to 118% by 2035 and surpassing WWII peak of 106% by 2029. OBBBA added $2.4T to 10-year deficits. Net interest at $970B exceeds defence spending and is rising as low-coupon debt rolls over. The debt ceiling (~November 2026) will become a political flashpoint during midterm season. T-bill supply at 21.9% of outstanding (above TBAC 15–20% range) pressures money markets. The twin deficit reinforcing dynamic and increasing domestic absorption of issuance creates a slow but persistent USD headwind.
key_data_points: CBO: debt/GDP 118% by 2035; surpasses WWII peak by 2029
OBBBA added $2.4T to 10-year deficits (CRFB)
Interest expense: $970B/year; each 100bps adds ~$100B annually
T-bill share: 21.9% (above TBAC 15–20% guideline)
Q1 2026 Treasury borrowing: $578B
Moody's downgrade: May 16, 2025 (last AAA removed)
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has priced gradual fiscal deterioration but NOT a fiscal catalyst event (auction failure, debt ceiling crisis, rating outlook change). The strategic edge is that fiscal deterioration is faster than the market's benign assumption. If TIC selling accelerates or a Treasury auction produces a significant tail, the fiscal risk premium would reprice abruptly.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral versus strategic bearish reflects fiscal risk's catalytic nature: it requires a trigger (auction failure, debt ceiling crisis) to translate from background liability to active market driver. No trigger exists within the tactical horizon, but several are building strategically.

tactical.analysis: DXY is in a complex technical environment: a structural downtrend (below 200-day MA at 101–103) with a sharp war-driven counter-trend rally from ~96 to 99.68. The MA configuration is bearishly stacked (200d > 100d > 50d; spot below all three), meaning systematic/CTA flows are positioned short USD. The war rally has taken DXY toward but not through the 50-day MA. Key levels bracket the range: support at 98 and 96; resistance at 99.68 and 100. Implied volatility has surged to ~11–13% for 1M DXY (pre-war ~7–8%), placing IV percentile above 80th. Risk reversal is modestly USD-call-bid reflecting war hedging. Realised vol likely exceeds implied vol, favouring spot over options. Vol term structure in backwardation (near-term war + FOMC premium).
key_data_points: 200-day MA: ~101–103 (spot below = bear trend intact)
MA configuration: bearishly stacked (200d > 100d > 50d)
DXY 1M implied vol: ~11–13% (pre-war ~7–8%)
IV percentile (1Y): >80th (elevated)
RV (20d): likely >IV (spot exceeding options expectations)
Key support: 98, 96; resistance: 99.68, 100, 101–103
tactical.what_is_priced: Options are pricing elevated near-term volatility consistent with war and FOMC cluster. The market has NOT priced a sustained break above 100 — put/call skew still favours USD puts structurally. Tactical edge is limited because the vol environment is correctly priced.

strategic.analysis: The structural picture is bearish USD. DXY's decline from ~110 to ~96 established a clear bear trend confirmed by bearishly stacked MAs and sustained trading below the 200-day MA. The war rally represents a counter-trend correction — historically, such corrections retrace 38–50% of the prior move before the trend resumes. The prior move was ~14 points, so a 38–50% retracement targets 101–103 (aligning with the 200-day MA). DXY at 99 has retraced approximately 21%. The BIS REER at 106.9 has corrected from 122.6 but remains above base — further real depreciation is consistent with fundamental convergence. Implied volatility will likely decline as the war premium fades, benefiting directional short-USD positions.
key_data_points: DXY structural trend: bear (110 → 96, -13%); war correction to 99 (~21% retracement)
Typical bear correction: 38–50% retracement → 101–103 (200-day MA zone)
BIS REER: 106.9 (still +7% above 2020 base)
Structural support: 95.5 (2025 low); 92–93 (bank consensus year-end)
IV trajectory: expected to decline from >80th toward 50th as war premium fades
strategic.what_is_priced: The market is pricing a gradual DXY decline toward low-to-mid 90s by year-end. The structural edge is modest: the bear trend is established and the 200-day MA at 101–103 provides a clear invalidation level. If DXY closes above 103 for 5+ sessions, the structural bear thesis needs reassessment.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral versus strategic bearish reflects the counter-trend correction within the structural downtrend. The war has created short-term noise that obscures but does not reverse the fundamental bearish trend. Once the event cluster resolves, the structural trend is expected to reassert.
factors.expression_assessment: Given the vol landscape: IV percentile above 80th means options are expensive — a directional short-USD view should be expressed via spot rather than long-put strategies. Realised vol exceeding implied vol confirms the market is moving more than priced, favouring spot. Vol term structure in backwardation suggests waiting for the event cluster to resolve (post-March 18 FOMC) before establishing directional positions, as vol will likely collapse post-event and the directional move will be cleaner. The 200-day MA at 101–103 is the stop/invalidation level for any short-USD position.

tactical.analysis: USD liquidity conditions are transitional with no strong tactical directional signal. The Fed ended QT on December 1, 2025, after a cumulative $2.4T reduction, and commenced RMPs at ~$40B/month in T-bill purchases. This pivot from liquidity withdrawal to injection is structurally USD-negative but the effect is gradual. Reserve balances at $3.004T are up $83B since RMPs but remain $361B below year-ago levels. ON RRP has collapsed to effectively zero, signalling all excess liquidity has been absorbed. SOFR at 3.65% with zero spread to IORB indicates healthy funding. TGA at ~$833–839B is declining, additive to system liquidity, but Tax Day in mid-April will reverse this. Cross-currency basis swaps have likely narrowed in Q1 2026 due to RMP-driven liquidity injection. Fed swap lines show zero usage — no acute offshore funding stress despite the war.
key_data_points: Fed balance sheet: $6.63T; QT ended December 1, 2025 (cumulative -$2.4T)
RMP pace: ~$40B/month T-bill purchases
Reserve balances: $3.004T (up $83B since RMPs; down $361B YoY)
ON RRP: effectively $0 (~$725M; collapsed from $2T+ peak)
SOFR: 3.65%; spread to IORB: ~0 bps
TGA: ~$833–839B (declining; liquidity-additive)
Fed swap line usage: $0
3M EUR/USD basis: estimated -10 to -15bps (narrowed)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the end of QT and start of RMPs. The tactical edge is minimal because the liquidity transition is proceeding as expected. The risk is that the Iran war creates unexpected USD funding demand from commodity hedging and margin calls, widening cross-currency basis swaps — but this has not materialised yet.

strategic.analysis: The strategic USD liquidity picture is bearish. The Fed's pivot from QT to RMPs represents a fundamental regime change in dollar supply. Over 6 months, RMPs at $40B/month will add approximately $240B to reserve balances, pushing toward $3.2–3.3T. This is USD-negative because it reduces the scarcity premium: during QT, global borrowers paid up for dollar funding, creating structural USD demand. That dynamic is now reversing. Cross-currency basis should continue narrowing, reducing hedging costs for foreign investors in USD assets — which paradoxically reduces foreign demand for USD. SLOOS shows continued net tightening in C&I lending but strengthening demand — the lending channel is constrained but not broken. The dollar smile framework places USD at the left side tactically, but the liquidity regime is pushing toward the bottom (ample liquidity → risk-on → weak USD) over the strategic horizon.
key_data_points: RMP injection: ~$40B/month → ~$240B over 6 months
ON RRP: effectively $0 (no further liquidity buffer to drain)
T-bill supply: 21.9% of outstanding (above TBAC 15–20% target)
SLOOS: C&I standards tightening; demand strengthening for large firms
Dollar smile: transitioning from left side toward bottom
Fed swap lines: $0 usage
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has priced the end of QT but not fully priced the positive liquidity impulse from 6+ months of RMPs. If EUR/USD 3M basis narrows from -10–15bps toward -5bps (pre-QT levels), the reduction in hedging costs makes EUR-denominated assets relatively more attractive, supporting the EUR/USD rally thesis. The strategic edge is that the liquidity regime change is a slow-moving but powerful force that markets tend to underestimate.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral versus strategic bearish reflects the lag between the liquidity regime change (QT → RMPs) and its full transmission to FX. The injection is happening but effects on cross-currency basis, risk appetite, and dollar supply take 3–6 months to fully materialise. Tactically, the war's USD funding demand provides a temporary offset.

weighted_score.interpretation: The net score of +0.031 is effectively zero — a genuine coin flip. The bullish factors (risk appetite, positioning, event risk) almost exactly offset the bearish factor (inflation/growth).
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Score 0.031 maps to Conviction 2/5 (Neutral) because the absolute value is below 0.10, indicating no meaningful directional signal.

tactical.rationale: The tactical picture is a genuine deadlock between two powerful opposing forces. On the bullish side, the Iran war has pushed USD to the left side of the dollar smile, drawing safe-haven flows and triggering aggressive short-covering from the near-5-year extreme net short position. CFTC shorts have trimmed $3.2B in a week, UUP inflows accelerated to the strongest pace since mid-2024, and VIX at 22–25 signals sustained risk-off demand for dollar liquidity. On the bearish side, the data flow is deteriorating rapidly: February NFP of -92K, JOLTS at post-pandemic lows, and Q4 GDP at +1.4% paint a picture of an economy rolling over. The US exceptionalism narrative has been shattered — the S&P Global Composite PMI differential with the Eurozone has closed to zero. The stagflationary signal from ISM Prices Paid at 70.5 alongside labour market weakness creates a policy dilemma not fully priced. The March 18 FOMC with fresh dot plot is the decisive near-term catalyst: a hawkish surprise would resolve the deadlock in USD's favour, a dovish surprise would break it bearish. Today's CPI adds another binary input. The path of least resistance is range-bound with mild bullish bias (DXY 98–101) because the war premium fades slowly and positioning squeeze has further to run, but the fundamental deterioration caps upside. Actionability is limited at Conviction 2/5 — this is a wait-for-catalyst environment.

g10_ranking.commentary: USD's mid-table ranking reflects equilibrium between safe-haven support and structural deterioration. USD loses to commodity-correlated high-yielders (NOK, AUD) benefiting from the oil price surge, and safe-haven peers (CHF, JPY) offering similar crisis protection without stagflationary drag. It beats low-yielders and trade-vulnerable currencies.

conditions: Iran war continues at current intensity without major escalation
March FOMC holds with one dot as median
February CPI in line with consensus (+0.3% MoM core)
base_case.path: DXY grinds within 98–100 as war premium slowly fades, offset by FOMC-related positioning. High intraday vol but limited net directional movement.

conditions: March dots shift to zero 2026 cuts or hot CPI (core >+0.4% MoM)
Cool CPI (core <+0.2% MoM) + March dots show two cuts

tactical.path_expectation: DXY is expected to chop within the 98–100 range for the next 2 weeks as the market processes the March FOMC and war headlines. The path is high intraday vol but limited net directional movement. CPI today may trigger a 50–70 pip EUR/USD move that quickly fades. The March 18 FOMC is the real inflection point — the fresh dot plot will either confirm or break the range. Post-FOMC, DXY is more likely to break lower than higher because fundamental forces outweigh the war premium unless escalation provides a new shock.

increase_to_5_if: Strait of Hormuz closed AND DXY closes above 103 for 5 days
Fed hikes AND US inflation >4% PCE
increase_to_4_if: March dots show zero 2026 cuts AND DXY closes above 101
War escalates to wider regional conflict AND VIX sustained above 35
decrease_to_3_if: March dots confirm one cut AND CPI in line
War status quo with no escalation for 2 weeks
decrease_to_2_if: Current state — balanced forces, no catalyst resolved yet
Mixed data flow continues without clear direction
decrease_to_1_if: Ceasefire AND cool CPI AND dovish FOMC dot plot simultaneously
DXY breaks below 96 AND positioning rebuilds short

risk_reward.downside_case_loss: DXY 99 → 102 = +3% (~300 DXY pips against a short)
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: No asymmetry. This is a balanced-risk environment with no skew. Not attractive for directional positioning at the tactical horizon.

weighted_score.interpretation: The net score of -0.548 is decisively bearish. Nine of ten factors are bearish at the strategic horizon, with only Event Risk neutral.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Score -0.548 maps to Conviction 3/5 (score between -0.40 and -0.60) because breadth is impressive but average confidence is moderate (55–60%).

strategic.rationale: The strategic case for USD weakness is comprehensive and multi-factor, representing the broadest bearish alignment in the 10-factor framework. The dominant driver is convergence of rate differentials (Factor 1, weight 0.24): the Fed is heading toward 3.00–3.25% by year-end while the RBA hikes, BoJ normalises, and Norges Bank holds at 4.00%. The external balance (Factor 5, weight 0.16) reinforces: twin deficit at 8.8% of GDP, NIIP at -$27.6T, and declining foreign UST holdings represent structural funding vulnerability at the 3–6 month horizon. USD Liquidity (Factor 10, weight 0.12) provides the mechanism: the Fed's pivot from QT to RMPs injects ~$40B/month, narrowing cross-currency basis and reducing the scarcity premium. Guidance & Credibility (Factor 6, weight 0.12) amplifies through the Fed Chair transition. The stagflationary data mix (Factor 2) is the catalyst forcing the Fed's hand. The only non-bearish factor is Event Risk, where the war's strategic trajectory is genuinely uncertain. Bank consensus for EUR/USD at 1.20–1.25 by year-end aligns with our directional view, though we are more cautious on magnitude given war uncertainty. The path of least resistance is DXY declining from 99 toward 94–96 over 6 months, back-loaded in Q3 once war premium fades and new FOMC leadership is established. Actionability is moderate at Conviction 3/5 — direction clear but timing complicated by the war and Chair transition. Optimal expression: sell USD on rallies toward 200-day MA (101–103).

g10_ranking.commentary: USD's 8/11 ranking reflects the breadth of structural headwinds. Currencies that beat USD share at least two of: higher/rising rates, stronger external balances, and less fiscal vulnerability. USD beats only the small open economies and recession-bound CAD.

conditions: Fed delivers one cut (June or September)
base_case.path: DXY chops at 98–100 through April, breaks lower in May (Powell departure), accelerates to 94–96 by September as the new rate path is established.

conditions: Iran war escalates to wider regional conflict
US inflation re-accelerates above 3.5% PCE forcing hawkish pivot
European growth disappoints fiscal stimulus expectations
War ends rapidly + oil retraces to $60
Treasury auction failure or debt ceiling confrontation

strategic.path_expectation: The expected path is a gradual, grinding USD decline rather than a sharp sell-off. Phase 1 (March–April): DXY consolidates at 98–100 as the market processes FOMC and war headlines. Phase 2 (May–June): DXY breaks lower to 95–97 as Powell's departure triggers institutional uncertainty and the first cut is delivered or firmly priced. Phase 3 (July–September): DXY grinds toward 94–95 as rate differential narrowing accelerates and war premium dissipates. Volatility expected to decline from current elevated levels toward the 40th–60th percentile. Pullbacks to 98–99 during the decline should be sold, with the 200-day MA (101–103) as ultimate invalidation.

increase_to_5_if: Fed delivers 50bp cut at any meeting
Foreign UST selling accelerates (TIC net outflows >$100B/quarter)
decrease_to_3_if: Current state — broadly bearish alignment with moderate confidence
War continues at current intensity without resolution
decrease_to_2_if: DXY closes above 200-day MA for 5+ sessions
decrease_to_1_if: US exceptionalism reasserts (US Composite PMI >55 while EZ <48)

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Mildly favourable. The distribution is modestly skewed bearish because structural forces (9 of 10 factors bearish) are more persistent than the geopolitical safe-haven bid. The aggressive downside scenario (DXY to 90–93) offers 600–900 pips versus the upside scenario (DXY to 102, +300 pips).

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: Banks overwhelmingly project EUR/USD at 1.20–1.25 by year-end (DXY ~93–96). The consensus driver is fading US exceptionalism, narrowing rate differentials, and European fiscal stimulus. Most banks expect 2–4 Fed cuts in 2026.
consensus_divergence.our_view: DXY 94–96 by September 2026. Direction aligned with consensus; magnitude slightly more conservative. Conviction at 3/5 is lower than the sell-side consensus's implicit high-conviction short-USD call.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Minimal on direction, moderate on confidence. We assign higher probability (25% vs consensus ~10–15%) to the war-escalation upside scenario.
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We assign higher probability to the war-escalation upside scenario because the Iran war is the most significant US military engagement since Iraq 2003 and the consensus is treating it as a 2–3 week event. If the war extends into Q3 or escalates to involve the Strait of Hormuz, the safe-haven bid could persist far longer than consensus assumes. We also assign more weight to the Chair transition risk — the market has not priced the institutional disruption of moving from Powell to Warsh.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: Short-USD positions at current levels yield 4.0% (400 pips DXY equivalent) by September, with the bulk of the move in Q2–Q3.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: Missing the faster decline from 99 to 93–94 costs 100–200 pips of alpha versus running the position from today. The opportunity cost of caution is modest.

cleanest_bullish_expression.pair: N/A — no bullish USD expression recommended at strategic horizon
cleanest_bullish_expression.rationale: With 9 of 10 strategic factors bearish, there is no clean bullish USD expression. If forced to express a tactical bullish view (war escalation), USD/CHF or USD/JPY offer the most carry support, but both face structural headwinds from safe-haven competition and BoJ tightening respectively.

cleanest_bearish_expression.rationale: BoJ is the only G10 central bank actively tightening while the Fed eases. The UST-JGB 10Y spread is narrowing structurally (from +250 to +207, projected toward +150). PPP suggests USD/JPY at 158 is 63% overvalued — the largest G10 distortion. Risk: BoJ surprises dovish or war escalation sends JPY weaker on oil import costs.

relative_value_pairs.thesis: German fiscal stimulus provides structural tailwind; UST-Bund spread compressing; EUR/USD near PPP fair value (~1.20 vs 1.16); ECB stability contrasts with Fed uncertainty.
RBA hiking while RBNZ holds; Australia CA surplus (+1.0%) vs NZ deficit (-6.5%); USD-neutral expression of Antipodean divergence.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Tactical neutral and strategic bearish are directionally consistent — neither horizon is bullish USD — but diverge on the timing and confidence of the bearish thesis.
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: The tactical neutrality reflects the Iran war's safe-haven bid and March FOMC uncertainty — temporary factors with identifiable expiration dates. Strategically, nine of ten factors are bearish, representing the broadest alignment in the framework. The divergence is timing and magnitude, not direction.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: March 18 FOMC dot plot or ceasefire announcement — whichever comes first
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: A dovish March dot plot (two+ cuts) combined with war de-escalation would convert the tactical stance from neutral to bearish, aligning both horizons. A hawkish surprise would extend the neutral phase.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: The cross-horizon alignment supports a 'wait and sell' approach: remain flat tactically, use DXY rallies toward 100–101 (or a post-FOMC dovish reaction) to establish strategic short-USD positions with a 200-day MA stop at 103.

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: USD's positive carry against EUR (the largest DXY component) means DXY forwards are slightly below spot — the market is pricing a gradual USD decline consistent with rate differentials. Our expected DXY at each horizon is more bearish than the forward, creating a quantifiable edge of 1–2.5 DXY points that increases with the horizon. The 6-month gap of ~2.5 points represents additional decline expected from structural factors beyond what carry alone implies.

key_catalysts.rationale: Consensus +0.3% MoM core, +2.5% YoY core. Hot print reinforces higher-for-longer and supports USD; cool print boosts June cut probability and weakens USD. Directly influences March FOMC dot plot.
Hold at 0.75% expected. Communication suggesting June hike to 1.00% would narrow UST-JGB differential and pressure USD/JPY.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Hold fully priced; June hike ~60% priced

key_catalysts.rationale: Updates the Fed's preferred inflation gauge. If core PCE stays at 3.0% or rises, hawkish repricing follows. GDP revision from 1.4% could be revised lower or confirmed.

Fresh dot plot — first since December 2025. The median dot determines repricing: zero cuts (hawkish = DXY +1–2%), one cut (neutral), two+ cuts (dovish = DXY -1–2%). Powell's characterisation of labour market risks vs inflation is the qualitative signal.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Hold 95% priced; dot plot outcome NOT priced
key_catalysts.bilateral_relevance: All USD pairs; strongest on EUR/USD and USD/JPY

key_catalysts.rationale: Hold at 2.00% expected. Any hawkish shift supporting EUR would widen relative attractiveness. Simultaneous FOMC and ECB creates paired binary risk.
Cluster of three G10 decisions. BoE ~50/50 hold/cut — a cut would be GBP-negative and modestly USD-positive. SNB and Riksbank expected to hold.
Hold at 4.00% expected. June cut guidance would impact USD/NOK.
~80% probability of hike to 4.10%. A hike narrows the AUD-USD rate differential, supporting AUD and weakening USD bilaterally.

key_catalysts.event: Powell Term as Fed Chair Expires
key_catalysts.rationale: Transition from Powell to Warsh creates communication vacuum and policy uncertainty. Markets dislike leadership transitions at major central banks.

key_catalysts.event: FOMC Decision + SEP (potentially first under Warsh)
key_catalysts.rationale: If Warsh chairs this meeting, the first dot plot under his leadership signals the new reaction function. Hawkish tilt supports USD; dovish pivot weakens it.
Renegotiation introduces bilateral tariff and trade uncertainty for North American FX.

key_catalysts.event: FOMC Decision + SEP (most likely first cut date)
key_catalysts.rationale: Market pricing suggests September as the most likely first 2026 cut. Delivery confirms easing cycle, weakening USD through the expectations channel.
key_catalysts.priced_in: ~47% probability of a cut by this meeting
key_catalysts.bilateral_relevance: All USD pairs; particularly EUR/USD and USD/JPY

key_catalysts.rationale: Fiscal policy uncertainty intensifies as election approaches. Historical pattern: USD weakens into midterms on policy uncertainty, then stabilises.

invalidators.condition: Core PCE rises above 3.5% YoY for two consecutive months
invalidators.mechanism: The Fed would be forced to consider hiking, widening rate differentials in USD's favour and removing any expectation of easing.

invalidators.condition: DXY closes above 200-day MA (~103) for 5 consecutive sessions
invalidators.mechanism: The structural downtrend would be broken and systematic/CTA flows would flip from short to long USD, creating a self-reinforcing rally.

invalidators.condition: Iran war escalates to full Strait of Hormuz closure sustained >2 weeks
invalidators.mechanism: Energy supply shock and flight to safety would overwhelm all structural bearish factors, sending DXY toward 104–108 analogous to 2022 Ukraine shock.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: WTI crude oil price and Hormuz shipping data

invalidators.condition: Eurozone Composite PMI falls below 48 for two consecutive months
invalidators.mechanism: The closing of the US-ROW growth gap — the core bearish thesis — would reverse, restoring US exceptionalism.

invalidators.condition: Fed funds futures reprice to show zero cuts for all of 2026 AND 2027
invalidators.mechanism: Rate differential advantage would persist and potentially widen, attracting carry-seeking capital back into USD.

invalidators.condition: Foreign official UST holdings increase by $100B+ in a single quarter
invalidators.mechanism: Would signal reversal of reserve diversification trend and renewed foreign demand for USD assets.

signposts.threshold_confirms: DXY remains below and MA slopes downward
signposts.threshold_invalidates: DXY closes above for 5+ consecutive sessions

signposts.threshold_confirms: Falls below 60 (inflation easing, Fed room to cut)

signposts.indicator: CFTC Aggregate Net USD Speculative Positioning

factors_changed.reason: Iran war transformed risk environment from goldilocks-adjacent to acute risk-off, moving USD to left side of dollar smile
War-driven short-covering trimmed net USD short from near-record levels; UUP inflows accelerated
Iran war is the dominant new catalyst — safe-haven demand directly supports USD

signposts_triggered.outcome: February NFP printed -92K (3rd negative in 5 months)
signposts_triggered.implication: Confirms labour market deterioration thesis; supports strategic bearish USD case
Confirms US exceptionalism unwind — core strategic bearish driver validated

signposts_triggered.outcome: Surged from ~$63 to $83–91 on Iran war
signposts_triggered.implication: New dynamic not anticipated in previous report; exacerbates stagflation concern

conviction_change.explanation: Tactical conviction decreased from 3 to 2 because the Iran war introduced a powerful safe-haven bid that neutralised the previously bearish tactical stance. Strategic conviction held at 3 because the structural drivers are unchanged or strengthening, but timing uncertainty from the war prevents an upgrade.

new_catalysts: Iran war (Operation Epic Fury, launched February 28) — most significant change since previous report
Supreme Court IEEPA tariff ruling (February 20) — reduced tariff risk but introduced new trade policy uncertainty
March 18 FOMC with dot plot — first data-informed dots since the February NFP shock

seasonality.pattern: USD historically shows a modest seasonal weakening tendency in Q2 (April–June), driven by US multinational repatriation for Tax Day (positive March, then fading) and Q1-to-Q2 portfolio rebalancing into international equities.
seasonality.historical_magnitude: Typically 1–2% DXY seasonal softness in Q2
seasonality.current_alignment: Aligns with the bearish strategic thesis — provides a mild tailwind for USD weakness in May–June. However, the Iran war's impact on seasonal patterns is unpredictable and safe-haven demand can override seasonal tendencies.

assumptions: BIS REER data is January 2026 (latest available). PPP deviations are from the 2023/2024 OECD vintage.
CFTC positioning data as of March 3, 2026 (released March 7). One-week lag means latest war-driven flows may not be fully captured.
Implied volatility, IV percentile, risk reversals, and cross-currency basis swap levels are estimated from public commentary — exact figures require Bloomberg terminal verification before trading.
DXY forward points are imputed from component pair forwards (primarily EUR/USD). Exact forward outright levels are rate-differential-based estimates.
War scenario probabilities are subjectively assigned based on Trump administration rhetoric, military analysis in tier-1 media, and historical conflict duration patterns.
G10+SGD macro data uses a combination of Q3/Q4 2025 readings where Q1 2026 data is not yet available, particularly for smaller economies.
Rate path projections combine OIS-implied pricing with our assessment of each central bank's reaction function and carry significant uncertainty given the Iran war's macro impact.
